coredata.dc:title: CQD-mediated homogeneous covalent organic framework membranes for ultrafast molecular sieving
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fan
Yang, Qiangqiang
Guo, Jing
Tan, Xiaoyu
Huang, Junhui
Zhang, Yanqiu
Shao, Lu
coredata.dc:description: Self-standing COF membranes with precisely defined channels enable selective separation and rapid solvent transport, showing the potential to overcome the trade-off between selectivity and permeability. However, self-standing COF membranes formed through conventional interfacial polymerization often suffer from structural heterogeneity, resulting in numerous gaps and defects that impair membrane integrity and separation performance. In this work, we present a simple method to increase the structural homogeneity of COF membranes via a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-mediated interfacial polymerization process. CQDs controllably release amine monomers at the reaction interface, enabling the COF layer to nucleate and grow slowly, resulting in a highly ordered and uniform membrane structure. The resulting COF‒membrane composite membranes, characterized by uniform, dense, and smooth surfaces, achieve precise separation between dyes and salts and exhibit an exceptionally high water flux of 92.1 L/m2 h bar. Furthermore, the separation process of the COF membrane aligns with a pore flow model, where the permeation flux for different solvents inversely correlates with their viscosity, reaching up to 213.6 L/m2·h·bar for acetonitrile. This work offers a promising strategy for the fabrication of highly crystalline and structurally precise COF membranes for energy-efficient environmental remediation and resource recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Interfacial polymerization
Covalent organic framework

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the TpMDA membrane and powder
Preparation of the CQD/TpMDA membrane and powder
Preparation of TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA powders via solvothermal synthesis
Pore size and pore size distribution measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication process for self-standing COF membranes. (a) Fabrication and morphology of the TpMDA membrane via pristine interfacial polymerization. (b) Fabrication and morphology of the CQD/TpMDA membrane via CQD-mediated interfacial polymerization.

Morphology characterization of the TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA membranes. (a) Cross-sectional morphology, (b) surface SEM morphology and (c) AFM morphology of the TpMDA membrane and the CQD/TpMDA membranes with different CQD additions.

Crystallization and surface characterization of the TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA membranes. (a) XRD spectra of TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA powders with different amounts of CQD added. (b) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore width of the TpMDA powder. (c) XRD spectra of Tp, MDA and CQD. (d) Schematic representation of the TpMDA structure. (e) Water contact angles and (f) zeta potentials of the TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA membranes with different CQD additions.

Membrane separation performance. (a) Separation performance comparison of the TpMDA membrane and the CQD/TpMDA membranes with different amounts of added CQDs for the removal of CR (100 ppm). (b) The permeance of the 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane plotted against 1/η. (c) Separation performance of the 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane for various dyes. (d) Performance comparison of the 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane with state-of-the-art membranes. (e) Sieving performance of the 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane for CR dye (100 ppm) and NaCl at different concentrations. (f) 40 h separation performance of the TpMDA membrane and 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane for CR dye.

head.ce:title: CQD-mediated homogeneous covalent organic framework membranes for ultrafast molecular sieving

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Membrane Separations, Adsorption, Catalysis and Spectroscopy for Sustainable Solutions (cMACS), KU Leuven, Celestijnenlaan 200F, 3001, Leuven, Belgium

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Catalysis and Spectroscopy for Sustainable Solutions (cMACS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Membrane Separations, Adsorption, Catalysis and Spectroscopy for Sustainable Solutions (cMACS), KU Leuven, Celestijnenlaan 200F, 3001 Leuven, Belgium.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-standing COF membranes with precisely defined channels enable selective separation and rapid solvent transport, showing the potential to overcome the trade-off between selectivity and permeability. However, self-standing COF membranes formed through conventional interfacial polymerization often suffer from structural heterogeneity, resulting in numerous gaps and defects that impair membrane integrity and separation performance. In this work, we present a simple method to increase the structural homogeneity of COF membranes via a carbon quantum dot (CQD)-mediated interfacial polymerization process. CQDs controllably release amine monomers at the reaction interface, enabling the COF layer to nucleate and grow slowly, resulting in a highly ordered and uniform membrane structure. The resulting COF‒membrane composite membranes, characterized by uniform, dense, and smooth surfaces, achieve precise separation between dyes and salts and exhibit an exceptionally high water flux of 92.1 L/m2 h bar. Furthermore, the separation process of the COF membrane aligns with a pore flow model, where the permeation flux for different solvents inversely correlates with their viscosity, reaching up to 213.6 L/m2·h·bar for acetonitrile. This work offers a promising strategy for the fabrication of highly crystalline and structurally precise COF membranes for energy-efficient environmental remediation and resource recovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenge of addressing global water shortages will intensify with population growth and escalating water pollution. The development of highly efficient, energy-saving technologies for water purification and wastewater recycling is essential [1–3]. For example, membrane-based separation methods have shown significant potential for advancing water treatment efforts across different applications. To realize the full potential of advanced separation technologies, it is crucial to design novel membrane materials that enhance separation performance while simultaneously minimizing costs and increasing processing efficiency [4,5]. Currently, commonly utilized amorphous polymer membranes possess irregular pore architectures and heterogeneous pore size distributions, which inherently impose a trade-off between selectivity and permeability [6]. This trade-off arises primarily from the complex, tortuous pathways formed by the densely cross-linked polymer chains, which heavily restrict water transport. In addition to conventional polymeric membranes, a variety of porous crystalline materials, including zeolites, metal‒organic frameworks (MOFs), and covalent organic frameworks (COFs), have shown significant potential for molecular separation because of their inherent porosity, low mass density, well-defined topology, permanent porosity, and structural versatility [7,8]. However, most zeolite- or MOF-based membranes are produced by either integrating these fillers into a polymer matrix or growing them on a substrate, which generally results in reduced permeance and suboptimal separation performance [9,10]. This diminished efficacy is likely due to polymer chains obstructing the porous fillers within the hybrid structure or due to inadequate crystal growth and poor adherence to the substrate [11,12]. COFs, in particular, present an advantageous, entirely organic, metal-free backbone that imparts structural flexibility [13–15]. This feature enables the fabrication of self-standing COF membranes through interfacial polymerization, optimizing the utilization of their ordered pore channels. Despite this promise, achieving a defect-free self-standing membrane from crystalline materials presents substantial challenges, mainly due to the uncontrollable stacking of COFs and the occurrence of grain boundary defects [16,17].

Interfacial polymerization continues to be the dominant technique for producing commercial reverse osmosis and nanofiltration membranes, primarily owing to its scalability and practical application potential. Similarly, the strategy of conducting interface-confined reactions has been explored for the fabrication of COF membranes [18]. To minimize defects in self-supported COF membranes prepared via the interfacial polymerization method, Jiang et al. employed Xe lamp irradiation to accelerate the tautomerization of reversible enol-imine linkages into the keto-enamine form, effectively reducing the presence of reversible enol-imine linkages and thereby expanding the amorphous regions [19]. These amorphous domains serve to link highly crystalline regions tightly and flexibly, effectively mitigating defects within the crystalline regions [20]. However, this approach may come at the cost of some permeance and a reduction in membrane structural uniformity. Improving the nanoscale homogeneity of self-supporting COF membranes while ensuring that the membrane remains defect-free is expected to be important for overcoming the permeability‒selectivity trade-off [21]. The stability of the interface between the organic and aqueous phases, namely, the precursor reaction zone, is critical to the homogeneity of the COF membrane. Chung et al. reported a method in which aldehyde and amine monomers were codissolved in the organic phase, while the catalyst was introduced into the aqueous phase. This configuration facilitated the gradual diffusion of the catalyst to the interface, effectively avoiding instability and excessive interfacial reaction rates. As a result, this approach enables the synthesis of uniform and defect-free COF membranes under ambient conditions [22]. However, dissolving both amine and aldehyde monomers in the same organic phase can result in some monomer loss. Interfacial polymerization is widely accepted as a diffusion-limited process. The stability of the reaction interface is primarily controlled by the diffusion of reactive monomers to the reaction zone. Therefore, regulating the diffusion of monomers in interfacial polymerization provides insight into the fabrication of defect-free and structurally uniform self-supporting COF membranes.

In this work, we devised a CQD-mediated interfacial polymerization strategy to regulate the diffusion of amine monomers from the aqueous phase to the reaction interface, thereby synthesizing a smooth, uniform, and defect-free self-supporting COF membrane at room temperature. The hydrophilic CQDs contained abundant –COOH and –NH/-NH2 groups. Rather than using free amine in the aqueous phase, the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between the CQD and amine monomers modulate the diffusion rate. This reduced diffusion rate of precursors across the interface enables a kinetically controlled interfacial reaction. Through this method, we successfully synthesized highly crystalline and uniform COF membranes with thicknesses varying from 1.1 μm to 2.5 μm. These COF membranes are highly flexible and can be transferred onto a substrate without structural damage. Additionally, we successfully extended this strategy to synthesize other COF membranes with different amine monomers. The newly developed COF membranes, with ultrahigh permeance and precise molecular and ion sieving properties, were applied for ultrafast, energy-efficient nanofiltration.

The PAN substrate was purchased from Rising Sun Membrane Technology (Beijing) Co., Ltd. N04607, N,1,3,5-Triformylphloroglucinol (Tp) and 2,2-bipyridine-5,5′-diamine (Bpy) were prepared from Jilin Chinese Academy of Sciences-Yanshen Technology Co. Ltd., and 4,4′-Methylenedianiline (MDA), methyl blue (MB), MgSO4 and MgCl2 were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. BTB (bromothymol blue) was obtained from Sigma‒Aldrich. Crystal violet (CV), Rose Bengal (RB), Congo Red (CR), methylene blue (MeB), bovine serum albumin (BSA), humic acid (HA), Na2SO4, NaCl, Evans blue (EB), Rhodamine B (RhB) and methyl orange (MO) citric acid and diethylenetriamine were obtained from Aladdin Company (China). Ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, dichloromethane (DCM) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were obtained from Xilong Science Company (China).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4500) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Multimode 8) were used to characterize the surface morphology and roughness of the membranes. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30) was used to analyze the degree of membrane crystallization. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 8700) was used to examine the chemical structure, whereas X-ray diffraction (XRD, BRUKER) was used to determine the crystallinity. Gas adsorption isotherms were measured via a Micrometrics ASAP 2420. The contact angles were evaluated via SL 200 KB, and the zeta potentials were analyzed via a SurPASS electrokinetic analyzer. UV–vis spectroscopy (Cintra20-GBC) was used to determine the dye solution concentration. The size distribution of the CQDs in solution was determined via dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malven ZS90).

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method [23,24]. Specifically, 0.96 g of citric acid and 540 μL of diethylenetriamine were dissolved in 2.0 g of deionized (DI) water and subjected to ultrasonic treatment at room temperature for 5 min. The resulting solution was then heated in a microwave oven at 750 W for 5 min. To remove residual small molecules, the obtained products were purified by dialysis using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 300 Da for 48 h in DI water. Finally, the CQD aqueous solution was freeze-dried for 48 h and stored in a dry environment for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the TpMDA membrane and powder
ce:section.ce:para: Self-standing and flexible COF nanofilms were synthesized through an interfacial reaction between aldehyde and amine monomers performed at ambient temperature and normal atmospheric pressure. In the conventional interfacial polymerization approach, a solution of Tp was prepared in dichloromethane at a concentration of 0.20 mmol/100 mL. The MDA solutions were prepared in a 70:30 (v/v) water/acetonitrile mixture at a concentration of 0.30 mmol/100 mL. The interfacial polymerization process was carried out in a beaker. Initially, 100 mL of the Tp solution was introduced into the beaker, followed by the addition of 50 mL of the 70:30 (v/v) water/acetonitrile mixture, which served as a spacer layer. Subsequently, 100 mL of the MDA solution was carefully and evenly added along the inner edge of the beaker to rest atop the spacer layer. The reaction mixture was left undisturbed at room temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 96 h, allowing the TpMDA film to form at the interface. Once the reaction was complete, the resulting free-standing thin film was gently removed via tweezers, thoroughly rinsed multiple times with deionized water and acetone to eliminate residual monomers, and either transferred onto a PAN substrate or stored in ultrapure water for subsequent analysis and characterization. TpMDA powder was obtained through the same preparation process by collecting the precipitate and interfacial solid layer in the system. It was then sequentially washed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm using dichloromethane, acetonitrile, water, and ethanol, followed by drying, grinding, and storing in a dry environment for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CQD/TpMDA membrane and powder
ce:section.ce:para: CQD/TpMDA thin films were also fabricated via an interfacial polymerization approach. The main difference lies in the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the amine monomer solution to regulate the diffusion of the amine monomer toward the reaction interface. A solution of Tp was prepared in dichloromethane at a concentration of 0.20 mmol/100 mL. The solutions of MDA and CQD were prepared in a 70:30 (v/v) water:acetonitrile mixture at concentrations of 0.30 mmol/100 mL and 0.01 to 0.04 wt%, respectively. The interfacial polymerization process was the same as that used for preparing the TpMDA film. The reaction mixture was left undisturbed at ambient temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 12–96 h, allowing the CQD/TpMDA film to form at the interface. After the reaction, the resulting free-standing thin film was gently removed via tweezers, thoroughly rinsed with deionized water and acetone to remove residual monomers, transferred to a PAN substrate or preserved in ultrapure water for further analysis and characterization. CQD/TpMDA powder was obtained through the same preparation process by collecting the precipitate and interfacial solid layer in the system. It was then sequentially washed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm using dichloromethane, acetonitrile, water, and ethanol, followed by drying, grinding, and storing in a dry environment for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA powders via solvothermal synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The Schiff base COF powders were synthesized following a solvothermal method adapted from the literature [15]. Specifically, Tp (0.3 mmol) and MDA (0.45 mmol) were added to a Pyrex tube along with 1.5 mL of mesitylene, 1.5 mL of dioxane, and 1.0 mL of either deionized water or an aqueous CQD solution. The resulting mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min to ensure uniform dispersion. The reaction tube was subsequently rapidly frozen in a liquid nitrogen bath (77 K), followed by three consecutive freeze–pump–thaw cycles to remove dissolved gases. After sealing under vacuum, the tube was heated at 120 °C for 72 h to facilitate the polymerization reaction. Upon completion, a red precipitate was obtained and separated by either centrifugation or vacuum filtration. The product was thoroughly washed with anhydrous acetone to remove residual monomers and oligomers. Further purification was achieved by solvent exchange using acetonitrile and anhydrous acetone 5–6 times. The resulting material was then dried under vacuum at 180 °C for 24 h and ground into a fine powder for storage under vacuum.

Permeation tests were conducted via a custom-built nanofiltration system with a membrane area of 12.50 cm2 operating at 1 bar pressure supplied by nitrogen. Various dye solutions and saltwater solutions, including EB, CR, RB, MB, RhB, BTB, MO, MeB, and CV (100 ppm) and MgSO4, MgCl2, Na2SO4, and NaCl (1000 ppm), were filtered through the system. To minimize concentration polarization, continuous stirring at 500 rpm was applied. Before each test, the membranes were incubated for approximately 30 min to achieve a steady state. The permeation flux was determined via Eq. (1).
(1)
P = V / A t
where P (L m−2 h−1 bar−1) is the membrane permeation flux, V (L) is the volume of pure water or solution penetrating through the membrane during operation time t (h), and A (m2) is the effective area of the membrane.

The rejection rates of the dye solutions were calculated via Eq. (2):
(2)
R =
(
1 −
C p
C f
)
× 100 %
where R is the rejection C
f and C
p are the solute concentrations in the feed and permeate solutions, respectively. The different dye concentrations were determined via UV–vis at specific wavelengths. The conductivity of the saltwater solution was analyzed via a conductivity meter (INESA Scientific Instrument, DDB-303A, China). The separation factor (α) was calculated on the basis of the rejection of dye (R dye) or salt (R salt) according to the following equation:
(3)
α =
R
d y e
/
R
S a l t
where R dye and R salt represent the rejection rates of the dye and salt, respectively. A higher separation factor indicates better selectivity of the membrane for dye over salt, which is essential for efficient dye/salt fractionation.

The antifouling properties of the COF membranes were evaluated through ternary-cycle filtration tests using HA and BSA as model foulants. Initially, the membrane was compacted with DI water at 1 bar pressure for approximately 30 min to achieve stability, and the initial pure water flux (J
w0 , in L·m−2·h−1 bar−1) was determined. A 1 g/L BSA (or HA) solution was then used as the feed for the fouling tests, with the corresponding permeation flux recorded as J
w1 (in L·m−2·h−1 bar−1). The J
w1 /J
w0 ratio indicates that the flux decreases due to fouling. Afterward, the membrane was rinsed with DI water, and the restored pure water flux J
w2 (in L·m−2·h−1 bar−1) was measured to determine the flux recovery ratio. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) was calculated via the following equation:
(4)
F R R =
J w 2
J w 0
× 100 %

The experiment was repeated three times, and J
w1 /J
w0 and J
w2 /J
w0 were recorded during each cycle. Additionally, D
Rr and D
Rir represent the reversible and irreversible fouling ratios, respectively, whereas the total fouling ratio (D
Rt ) is their sum, which is calculated via specific equations.
(5)
R t
=

)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pore size and pore size distribution measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Solute rejection is considered to be dependent on solute size and is modeled via a log-normal probability function, as expressed in Eq. (8).
(8)
R T

where R

s
). It can be written as:
(9)
y =

where r
s represents the hydrated (Stokes) radius of the solute, μ
s is the geometric mean radius of the solute at RT = 50 %, and σ
g is the geometric standard deviation of μ
s, which is defined as the ratio of the solute radius at R
T = 84.13 % and R
T = 50 %. The values of σ
g and μ
s determine the sharpness and position of the rejection curves, respectively. The relationship between R
T and r
s can be expressed via Eq. (10).
(10)

)
= A + B

This equation provides the functional or fitted relationship between the rejection R
T and the solute radius r
s . F(⋅) represents a transformed form of R
T (for instance, connected to the error function in Eq. (8)). A and B are fitting or empirical constants that describe the position and slope of the rejection curve.

Neglecting the influence of steric and hydrodynamic interactions between solutes and membrane pores on solute rejection, the mean effective pore radius and geometric standard deviation are assumed to correspond to μ
s and σ
g . Consequently, the pore size distribution of NF membranes can be represented by the following probability density function:
(11)

where d
p is the pore size in diameter. where μ
p is the mean effective pore radius. σ
p is the geometric standard deviation.

PEGs (Mw = 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500 and 2000 g mol−1) were added to 0.2 g/L solutions to determine solute rejection. The Stokes radii (m) of the PEGs were determined on the basis of their molecular weights.
(12)
r s
= 16.73 × 1
0
− 12
×
M W
0.557
where r
s is the corresponding Stokes radius of the PEGs and M
W (g mol−1) is the molecular weight of the PEGs. The r
s values of PEGs with molecular weights of 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500 and 2000 g mol−1 are 0.32 nm, 0.47 nm, 0.59 nm, 0.32 nm, 0.69 nm, 0.78 nm, and 0.98 nm, respectively.

We developed a CQD-mediated interfacial polymerization strategy to fabricate self-standing COF membranes with fewer defects and more uniform pore channels. This was achieved by confining and regulating the diffusion of amine monomers to the water‒oil interface. In a conventional interfacial polymerization process for Schiff-based COF membrane synthesis, the interfacial reaction between aldehyde and amine monomers starts immediately and vigorously upon contact between the two phases, resulting in an unstable interfacial layer, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The resulting self-standing COF membrane exhibited a water-facing surface covered with randomly stacked nanospheres and an organic-facing surface with interwoven short rod-like structures, which were nonuniform and had significant defects (Fig. S1, Fig. S2). In our study, an aqueous solution containing CQD and 4,4′-methylenedianiline (MDA) was slowly added to an organic solution of 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp) in dichloromethane, allowing for interfacial reaction control (Fig. 1b). The CQDs were prepared via a one-pot, microwave-assisted condensation of citric acid and diethylenetriamine, with water serving as the solvent to ensure condensation without carbonization. We analyzed the surface functional groups of the synthesized CQDs via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and 1H NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S3). In the FTIR spectrum, the stretching bands at 1697 and 1542 cm−1 are attributed to the CO group of carboxylic acids and the N–H bond of amide linkages within the CQD structure. In the 1H NMR spectrum, the broad peak in the 3–4 ppm range is attributed to a high density of –CH2–N, –CH2–O, and –CH–OH groups, which are commonly found adjacent to amine, carboxyl, and aldehyde functionalities on the CQD surface. The broadening and overlapping of peaks are characteristic of the diverse functional groups and polydisperse nature of CQDs. Additionally, the broad signal observed in the 5.5–6 ppm region is associated mainly with –NH and –OH protons, with hydrogen bonding contributing to the broadness of the signal. These results confirm that the synthesized CQDs are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups. These functional groups can form weak interactions with MDA (containing –NH2) through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions, which do not result in strong covalent bonds; instead, they involve reversible physical or weak chemical interactions [25,26]. Additionally, dynamic light scattering (DLS) characterization revealed that the hydrodynamic diameter of the CQDs in aqueous solution ranges from 5 to 10 nm, whereas the molecular size of MDA is approximately 1 nm (Fig. S4). Consequently, CQDs can serve as a “controlled release” system for MDA, where a portion of MDA temporarily binds to the CQD surface and is gradually released in response to local concentration changes, enabling dynamic regulation of MDA diffusion and concentration. We employed the natural logarithm of the concentration ratio before and after diffusion to calculate the diffusion rates of the MDA monomer in both the MDA aqueous solution and the CQD/MDA aqueous solution. In the presence of CQDs, the diffusion coefficient of MDA decreased from 1.137 to 1.125 (Fig. S5). This regulation of diffusivity led to a more controlled and uniform distribution of the amine monomers at the interface, resulting in a smoother interfacial reaction, fewer random accumulations, and minimal defects in the final COF structure. Consequently, a more uniform water-facing surface and an interwoven long rod-like structured organic-facing surface of the COF membrane formed (Fig. S6). The chemical structures of the CQD-mediated COF membranes were characterized via FTIR (Fig. S7). The membrane exhibited characteristic C═O, C═C, and C─N stretching bands at 1598, 1578, and 1292 cm−1, confirming the successful formation of the keto-enamine-linked COF structure. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of the resulting COF membrane shows subtle changes at 1542 cm−1 (N–H in CQD) and 1697 cm−1 (CO in CQD) with increasing CQD content, confirming the presence of a small amount of carbon dots within the membrane structure.

To validate the importance of the CQDs, we synthesized and characterized a series of CQD/TpMDA membranes with varying CQD concentrations and reaction times. Compared with that of the pristine TpMDA membrane, the thickness of the 0.01CQD/TpMDA membrane increased from 1.4 μm to 2.4 μm. With further increases in the CQD concentration, the membrane thickness gradually stabilized at approximately 2.5 μm (Fig. 2 a). The increased thickness of the selective layer after CQD addition may be attributed to the interaction between the CQDs and MDA, which partially immobilizes MDA in the aqueous phase. This allows for its gradual release and slows its diffusion to the oil‒phase interface, thereby extending the reaction time window. When the reversible Schiff base reaction between MDA and Tp occurs at the water‒oil interface, an initial layer forms, gradually thickens, increases the monomer diffusion resistance, and eventually stabilizes. The reversible nature of the Schiff base (-CN-) bond allows dynamic self-repair and rearrangement [15,27]. When the monomer supply shifts from sudden influx to gradual release, the polymer layer at the interface undergoes continuous localized dissociation and reorganization over an extended timescale, leading to a thicker and more uniform membrane structure [28]. Under the CQD-assisted interfacial polymerization (IP) process, the slower, prolonged diffusion of MDA, combined with the reversible Schiff base reaction, provides more time and space for COF nucleation and growth, resulting in a thicker CQD/TpMDA layer. With increasing CQD concentration, SEM images of the membrane surfaces reveal a transition from initially defective surfaces with randomly stacked nanospheres to smoother and more uniform surface structures (Fig. 2b). The AFM images of the COF membrane surface show that the roughness decreases from 181 nm to 6.09 nm and then increases to 49.7 nm and 46.5 nm as the CQD concentration continues to rise (Fig. 2c). Pore size distribution tests further supported this finding, showing that, compared with the TpMDA membrane, the CQD/TpMDA membrane had a narrower pore size distribution and reduced effective pore sizes (Fig. S8). This is mainly attributed to the increased membrane thickness and more uniform membrane structure after CQD addition. Additionally, the thickness of the COF membranes was dependent on the reaction time and could be tuned from 1.1 μm to 2.5 μm by extending the interfacial reaction duration from 24 to 96 h, as illustrated in Fig. S9. At a reaction time of 96 h, the 0.02 CQD/TpMDA membrane exhibited superior rejection performance. Therefore, 96 h was selected as the optimized reaction time for further investigation (Fig. S10). Although reducing the concentration of MDA monomers can also slow their diffusion to the reaction interface, it simultaneously decreases the overall monomer supply. This often leads to membrane surface defects and poorer mechanical strength (Fig. S11), making it difficult to transfer the membrane onto the PAN substrate. In contrast, our CQD/TpMDA membranes exhibited sufficient mechanical robustness, allowing them to be easily removed from water and transferred onto PAN supports. Even after drying, the CQD/TpMDA membranes retained their flexibility and structural integrity, further demonstrating their mechanical durability (Fig. S12).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out to characterize the crystalline structure of the synthesized COF membranes. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the TpMDA membrane exhibited a prominent diffraction peak at a low 2θ angle of 6.7°, corresponding to the (100) plane reflection, which indicates a pore size of approximately 13.2 Å. This value aligns well with the pore width determined from the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) measurements (approximately 13.5 Å, Fig. 3b), suggesting the formation of an ordered porous structure. Notably, the double peak at 2θ = 6.7° closely matches the characteristic XRD peak of the Tp monomer (Fig. 3c), implying the possible presence of unreacted Tp monomers. In addition, the crystallization peak intensity of the CQD/TpMDA membrane is slightly lower than that of the pristine TpMDA membrane. This decrease may be attributed to the branched structure of the CQDs, which introduces significant steric hindrance and leads to more amorphous regions within the membrane. Importantly, the incorporation of CQDs does not shift the COF diffraction peaks, indicating that the crystalline framework of TpMDA remains intact. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images further confirmed that both TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA contain similar lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of approximately 4.9 Å (Fig. S13), indicating that the CQDs reside mainly in amorphous regions and do not alter the overall COF crystallinity. To probe the possible crystal packing of the COF, we synthesized the TpMDA COF via a thermal method and performed a simulated XRD analysis (Fig. S14). The results suggest that the possible COF structure is as illustrated in Fig. 3d. The high crystallinity of the CQD/TpMDA membrane ensures high porosity and good pore connectivity within the membrane structure, which facilitates rapid solvent transport and precise size-selective separation. Notably, the effective pore size of the CQD/TpMDA membrane (9 Å), on the basis of PEG solute retention calculations, is slightly smaller than the pore size indicated by the XRD result of the TpMDA COF (Fig. S8). This reduction is attributed primarily to the presence of multilayered and misaligned stacking in the COF membrane structure. Furthermore, the membrane structure significantly influences the surface hydrophilicity and charge properties. The water contact angles (WCAs) of the TpMDA and CQD/TpMDA membranes were measured to evaluate their hydrophilicity (Fig. 3e). The incorporation of CQDs significantly enhanced the hydrophilicity of the TpMDA membrane surface, as indicated by a reduction in the water contact angle from 54.7° to 25.1°. This improvement can be attributed to two key factors. First, the abundant hydrophilic carboxyl and amino functional groups present in the CQDs contributed to the increased water affinity. Second, the original TpMDA membrane surface exhibited irregularly stacked spherical structures, creating air gaps upon water contact, which led to a higher apparent contact angle according to the Cassie–Baxter model [29]. In contrast, the CQD/TpMDA membranes feature a smoother surface, allowing water to spread and penetrate more easily while reducing or eliminating trapped air pockets, thereby further lowering the contact angle. Furthermore, the CQD/TpMDA membrane exhibited a notably higher water adsorption capacity (Fig. S15), which is advantageous for minimizing pollutant adhesion and facilitating water transport through the membrane. To further investigate the surface charge properties, zeta potential measurements were conducted (Fig. 3f). The results revealed that all the membranes were negatively charged under neutral pH conditions, a characteristic that favors the rejection of anionic solutes.

Compared with the pristine COF membrane, the CQD/TpMDA membrane presented fewer defects due to the restricted diffusion of amine monomers during the interfacial polymerization process. As the CQD content increased, the rejection rate of the CQD/TpMDA membrane significantly improved (Fig. 4 a). At a CQD concentration of 0.01 wt% in the reaction system, the striated CQD/TpMDA membrane, with a uniform pore structure, achieved an impressive water permeance of 92.1 L m−2 h−1·bar−1 and a 96.2 % rejection rate for CR molecules. The permeation properties of the CQD/TpMDA membranes were studied in water and organic solvents with varying polarity gradients. The CQD/TpMDA membranes exhibit excellent permeance, such as for acetonitrile (213.6 L m−2 h−1 bar−1), methanol (163.2 L m−2 h−1 bar−1), water (92.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1), ethanol (71.3 L−2 h−1 bar−1), and isopropanol (46.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) (Fig. 4b). The permeance of the CQD/TpMDA membranes demonstrated a good linear relationship with 1/η (inverse viscosity), which was attributed to the structural rigidity and intrinsic porosity of the COF crystalline structure and the composite pore flow transport mechanism, where the solvent permeance is governed mainly by the solvent viscosity. The separation performance of the as-prepared membranes was evaluated using various dyes with different molecular weights, sizes, and charges as solutes (Fig. 4c, Fig. S16). The rejection of different dyes by the CQD/TpMDA membrane followed the following order: Evans blue (EB, 99.2 %) > Congo red (CR, 96.2 %) > Rose Bengal (RB, 94.4 %) > methyl blue (MB, 80.7 %) > rhodamine B (RhB, 72.1 %) > bromothymol blue (BTB, 69.3 %) > methyl orange (MO, 24.7 %) > crystal violet (CV, 15.2 %) > methylene blue (MeB, 11.5 %). This trend reflects the combined effects of size exclusion and electrostatic repulsion provided by the CQD/TpMDA membranes. The permeation flux slightly decreased during the separation of positively charged dyes such as CV and MeB, which may be attributed to the adsorption and pore blockage caused by dye molecules on the membrane surface. On the basis of the rejection performance for various dyes, the molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of the CQD/TpMDA membranes was estimated to be approximately 810 Da (Fig. S17). We compared the MWCO and water permeability of the prepared membranes with those of previously reported 3D COF membranes, 2D COF membranes, and polymer membranes, and the results demonstrated that the CQD/TpMDA membranes exhibited superior separation performance (Fig. 4d) [8,27,30–38]. Moreover, the CQD/TpMDA membrane resulted in low rejection rates for both monovalent and multivalent salts (<4 %), following the order of MgSO4 (3.8 %) > MgCl2 (3.1 %) > Na2SO4 (2.1 %) > NaCl (1.6 %) (Fig. S18). The results demonstrated that the CQD/TpMDA membrane exhibited the characteristic separation behavior of loose nanofiltration (loose-NF) membranes, achieving high dye rejection while maintaining low salt rejection. Its separation factor for CR and NaCl, defined as α CR/NaCl
R CR/RNaCl = 60.1, highlights its excellent selectivity. This property is particularly advantageous for recovering reusable resources from textile wastewater. To evaluate the separation performance of dyes from dye/salt mixtures, CR and NaCl were employed as model solutes (Fig. 4e). The membrane showed excellent dye separation selectivity, even at elevated salt concentrations of up to 3000 ppm. The precise sieving of membranes between dye molecules and salts can be attributed primarily to the highly crystalline and uniform COF selective layer, which establishes rapid and precise transport channels.

Membrane fouling is a bottleneck problem for performance degradation and reduced membrane lifetime as a result of the irreversible adsorption of various pollutants [39]. The anti-fouling property of the CQD/TpMDA membrane was measured by using humic acid (HA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) as probe foulants for three cycles. As shown in Fig. S19, the total fouling ratios (DRt) of the CQD/TpMDA membranes were 9.5 % for HA and 9.9 % for BSA. The permeation flux recovery ratios (FRR) were 93.9 % for HA and 91.6 % for BSA after three-cycle filtration tests. The outstanding antifouling properties of the CQD/TpMDA membrane were attributed to its highly hydrophilic and negatively charged surface, which helps mitigate the irreversible adsorption of BSA/HA molecules. COF materials are known to exhibit strong adsorption affinity for certain organic dyes commonly used in membrane performance evaluations, making it essential to exclude selective adsorption as the dominant separation mechanism in COF membranes [4,40]. To address this, we performed mass balance calculations for the solutes in the feed, permeate, and retentate solutions. This analysis aimed to determine whether the rejected dyes were retained in the retentate or adsorbed onto the COF membranes. The calculations revealed that only 2.83 % of the retained RB dye solute was adsorbed onto the membrane (Fig. S20). Furthermore, we conducted an RB adsorption equilibrium test over four cycles (with each cycle lasting 20 min). By the second cycle, the amount of dye adsorbed no longer increased, indicating that adsorption equilibrium under separation conditions was reached. On the basis of the long-term separation performance test over 40 h, the RB dye rejection percentage remained greater than 95 % before and after adsorption equilibrium. These results indicate that the rejection is attributed primarily to sieving rather than adsorption. In contrast, the 40-h separation performance test of the TpMDA membrane without CQDs revealed a significant decrease in permeance and retention (Fig. 4f). This may be due to dye clogging some of the uneven pores of the TpMDA membrane. Compared with the pristine TpMDA membrane, the CQD/TpMDA membrane exhibited greater flexibility and a smoother surface. These self-standing thin films were easily transferred to various substrates. More importantly, the CQD-assisted interfacial polymerization strategy can also be extended to the synthesis of other self-supporting Schiff-base COF membranes. For example, by replacing 4,4′-methylenedianiline (MDA) with 3,3′-bipyridine-6,6′-diamine, carbon quantum dots can still regulate monomer diffusion to form more uniform membrane structure COF membranes with fewer defects (Fig. S21). Notably, when p-phenylenediamine was used as the amine monomer, the self-supporting membrane prepared via CQD-assisted interfacial polymerization presented poor mechanical properties and was difficult to transfer (Fig. S22). This may be due to the shorter chain length of p-phenylenediamine, resulting in a COF with greater rigidity and lower flexibility. CQD-regulated interfacial polymerization to control monomer diffusion in the synthesis of self-supported COF membranes is a promising strategy for finely tuning various COF membrane structures.

In summary, a versatile synthetic methodology has been developed for the fabrication of COF membranes with a uniform surface, fewer defects and a highly crystalline structure via a CQD-mediated interfacial polymerization technique. The behavior of the CQDs facilitates the aggregation of amine monomers through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions, confining and regulating their diffusion into the reaction zone. This approach prevents intense interfacial reactions and uneven distribution of amine monomers, ultimately yielding smooth COF membranes with structural homogeneity. The resulting COF membranes, characterized by their well-defined pore structures, enable the precise sieving of dye molecules and salt ions. Moreover, their intrinsic porosity provides high-speed channels for solvent transport, achieving an acetonitrile permeance of 213.6 L/m2 h bar. The CQD-mediated strategy for regulating amine monomer diffusion to control the membrane structure can be extended to the preparation of other Schiff-base COFs via interfacial polymerization. This work offers valuable insights into the fabrication of COF membranes with uniform and well-ordered structures for use in molecular sieving applications.

Fan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qiangqiang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Investigation. Jing Guo: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaoyu Tan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Junhui Huang: Software, Formal analysis. Yanqiu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Lu Shao: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program (2023YFE0127000), National Natural Science Foundation of China (92475205, 22178076,22208072), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Ministry of Education (2023FRFK03047, HIT.OCEF.2024007), the Open Research Fund of Suzhou Laboratory (SZLAB-1308-2024-ZD007), Jiangsu Future Membrane Technology Innovation Center (BM2021804).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors have no conflict of interest.
